text: Drinking 8 glasses of water per day supports metabolism and reduces hunger. Water is essential for every cellular process in the body.
Drinking 500ml of water before meals reduces calorie intake by up to 13%. Water before eating makes you feel fuller sooner.
Dehydration reduces physical performance by up to 30% and slows metabolism. Aim for 2 to 3 litres of water per day based on your activity level.
Protein is the most satiating macronutrient. Eating 1.6 to 2.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight preserves muscle during weight loss.
Complex carbohydrates like oats, brown rice, and sweet potatoes provide sustained energy and fibre, which supports digestion and reduces cravings.
Healthy fats from avocado, nuts, and olive oil are essential for hormone production. Dietary fat does not directly cause body fat gain — calorie surplus does.
A calorie deficit of 500 kcal per day leads to approximately 0.45 kg of fat loss per week. One kilogram of fat stores roughly 7700 kcal.
Crash diets below 800 kcal per day cause muscle loss and metabolic adaptation. A moderate deficit of 300 to 500 kcal is sustainable and preserves lean mass.
Weight loss is not linear. Expect fluctuations of 1 to 2 kg from water retention, sodium intake, and hormonal changes. Focus on the 4-week trend, not daily numbers.
BMI below 18.5 is classified as underweight. BMI 18.5 to 24.9 is normal. BMI 25 to 29.9 is overweight. BMI 30 or above is obese.
BMI does not account for muscle mass. Athletes often have a high BMI but low body fat. Use BMI as one indicator alongside waist circumference and body fat percentage.
Reducing BMI by just one unit significantly reduces risk of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and sleep apnoea.
Resistance training 2 to 4 times per week builds muscle, which increases resting metabolic rate. More muscle means you burn more calories at rest.
Walking 10000 steps per day burns approximately 300 to 500 extra calories depending on body weight and pace. It is the most accessible form of daily activity.
HIIT sessions of 20 minutes burn as many calories as 45 minutes of steady-state cardio due to the afterburn effect known as EPOC.
Eating breakfast within 2 hours of waking regulates appetite hormones throughout the day. Skipping breakfast often leads to larger meals later.
Eating the majority of calories before 3pm is associated with better weight management. Late-night eating is linked to fat storage due to lower insulin sensitivity.
Spacing meals 3 to 5 hours apart allows insulin levels to drop between meals, which promotes fat burning. Constant snacking keeps insulin elevated and inhibits fat loss.
Poor sleep increases ghrelin the hunger hormone and decreases leptin the fullness hormone, causing an average of 385 extra calories consumed per day.
7 to 9 hours of sleep per night is optimal for weight loss. During deep sleep the body releases growth hormone, which repairs muscle and burns fat.
Sleep deprivation impairs glucose metabolism and increases cravings for high-calorie foods. Prioritising sleep is as important as diet and exercise for fat loss.
Green tea contains catechins and caffeine, which together increase fat oxidation by 16% and boost metabolic rate by 3 to 4%. Drink 2 to 3 cups daily for best effect.
The EGCG in green tea inhibits enzymes that break down norepinephrine, increasing fat cell breakdown. It is most effective when consumed before exercise.
Healthy snacks between meals prevent blood sugar crashes and overeating at main meals. Good options include Greek yoghurt, nuts, fruit, or boiled eggs.
